_comment: Page-cited intake findings. Each is a short, attributed factual extraction carrying a source PAGE reference for provenance. tools/check_provenance.py gates every entry.

findings.finding: Prior video-EEG series (Ochs 1984, Robillard 1983) had scored ipsiversion in 48-50% of seizures and concluded head turning had no lateralizing value; Wyllie attributes the discrepancy to their more permissive definition of 'versive' that included mild/ambiguous rotations. When only marked, sustained, unambiguous forced turning was scored as versive, ipsiversion was never observed (0/61).

Version's temporal relationship to other ictal signs differed by lobe of onset: 35/61 (57%) versive seizures began with the versive movement itself, and 91% of these were frontal/extratemporal; none were temporal. All 18 seizures in which staring-plus-automatisms preceded contraversion (30% of the versive cohort) arose from temporal onset, and contraversion never occurred as the initial sign in a temporal-onset seizure.

Of the 27/61 (44%) versive seizures that secondarily generalized, 12 (44%) ended with ipsiversion during or after the generalized convulsion - only the first, pre-generalization versive movement reliably lateralizes; turning observed during/after secondary generalization can reverse side and should not be used for lateralization.

Interictal EEG foci among the 27 patients with versive seizures were exclusively contralateral to the versive direction in 81% (22/27) and never exclusively ipsilateral (5/27 bilateral), corroborating the ictal contralateral-version finding.

Nonversive (mild, unsustained, wandering, or seemingly voluntary) head/eye turning occurred in 13 seizures from 10 patients and was split almost evenly between ipsilateral (7 seizures/4 patients) and contralateral (6 seizures/6 patients) to seizure onset, confirming it carries no lateralizing value.
